notes: Overall debate on the Phase 2 Tax Review proposals where Policy & Resources proposed a GST package but gave other options too. In the end all major changes were rejected.
Proposed by Prow/Dudley-Owen to ensure GST can only be changed by amending the law.
Proposed by Parkinson/McKenna to bring in a new territorial corporate tax regime, stem spending growth and then report by December 2029 if more changes are needed.
Proposed by Roffey/Ferbrache to help lower income pensioner couples.
Proposed by Soulsby/St Pier to establish a special investigation committees to review what can be done about spending and tax at the same time as cutting some spending.
Proposed by Gollop/Matthews to exempt food and charities from GST.
Proposed by Gollop/Matthews to increase the income tax allowance by £5,525 if GST came in.
Proposed by Matthews/Gollop to raise more money from property tax and agree in principal to a new way of calculating it based on property value.
Proposed by Burford/Trott to increase the cost of capital projects which can be approved by P&R without a report going to the States from £3m to £5m.
Proposed by Helyar/Ferbrache for large spendings cuts in Budgets between 2024 and 2028 should the main proposal be lost or measures introduced not raise at least £50m. to £60m.
Proposed by Le Tocq/Ferbrache
Proposed by Leadbeater/Cameron for a report on what is causing those who could work not to.
Proposed by Meerveld/Vermeulen to remove GST from all proposals.
Proposed by Ferbrache/Helyar setting out three options for the States to choose between, each to generate an extra £85m.

Proposed by Soulsby/St Pier for a package of measures without GST, other ways of raising income and a direction to costs reductions by £10m to £16m over 5 years, with a report after the election on if more action is needed.
Proposed by Roffey/Bury only if amendment 13 was successful, which was withdrawn.
Proposed by Roffey/Bury to further change the amended package if GST was removed to include raising extra £10m. from motoring taxes.
Proposed by Gollop/Matthews to remove a reference to plated meals in the GST exemptions.

Recognise that Guernsey’s public finances are not sustainable long-term and commits the government to urgently introduce phased spending controls, new revenue measures and reforms—especially to health and care—to secure the island’s future.

amendment_ref: OPTION A (As inserted by Amdt 14 AND amended by Amdts 5 & 17)
phase_label: Proposition 2 — Option A (inserted by Amdt 14; Amdts by A5 & A17)
notes: A package of revenue raising and cost reduction measures which included a 5% GST, with retail food and sales of books and clothes by charity exempted.

amendment_ref: Option B as inserted by Amdt 14
phase_label: Proposition 3 — Option B (as inserted by A14)
notes: Revenue raising to include £20m. from the corporate sector, a 50% increase in TRP, paid parking, service reductions totalling up £16m.

amendment_ref: Option C as inserted by Amdt 14
phase_label: Proposition 4 — Option C (as inserted by A14)
notes: Savings of up to £35m. a year by service reductions and raising an extra £20m. from the corporate sector

amendment_ref: Option D as inserted by Amdt 14
phase_label: Proposition 5 — Option D (as inserted by A14)
notes: Not to introduce a GST, but reduce spending and introduce other revenue raising including through TRP and increasing the current personal tax caps.

To agree that the challenge of agreeing a single package to resolve the forecast £85M medium term shortfall has proven too politically challenging so needs to be split into two tranches, with the first approved by this Assembly.
Review how much has already planned to be spent on major infrastructure projects and consider more borrowing for them.
Set up a committee to review how savings could be made to deliver up to £16m. over the next five years.
Direct P&R tp identify a longer-term vision for the island and report back by the end of 2024.
Set up a sub-group to investigate changes to the tax system.
Invetigate how much money is generated from transport and consider fuel duty, distance charging, paid parking and road tax.
Look at raising £2m. a year through a visitors' levy.
Produce an estimate of the structural deficit in every Budget.
Report back by September 2024 on changes to the public sector pension scheme and staff terms and conditions.
Review a law to make it easier to commission services, bringing back proposals by the end of 2023.
Increase P&R's delegated authority for approval of capital spending to £5m.
Report back by January 2024 with measures to get people who could be working but choose not to back into employment.
To use the Budget reserve to pay for all the reviews being called for.
P&R to report back by June 2026 on all the actions taken and the impact they have had on the financial position and what should follow.
Prepare any legislation necessary because of the States' decisions in this debate.

Overall debate on the Funding & Investment plan which scrapped the tax and benefits package agreed in February 2023 and sent P&R away to further review options for addressing the deficit and report back by September 2026.
Proposed by Roffey/Matthews to unbundle the original P&R package to allow individual votes on different issues.
Proposed by Roffey/de Sausmarez to borrow £150m. to lend to an affordable housing builder.
Proposed by St Pier/Vermeuelen to establish a special committee to carry out a root and branch review of the tax system.
Proposed by Soulsby/St Pier to continue a phased approach to tax reform.
Proposed by Kazantseva-Miller/de Sausmarez to review the level of spending on major projects and how it compares to GDP.
Proposed by Brouard/Bury so that under any scenario there can be a vote on agreeing that hospital modernisation continues.
Proposed by Parkinson/McKenna to implement a new territorial system of corporate income tax to raise £30m. a year.
Proposed by Matthews/Roffey.
Proposed by Trott/Gollop to increase personal income tax to 22% in 2024 and then up to 23% in 2025 should other revenue raising measures be defeated.
Proposed by Matthews/Roffey to stop Education's plans to build a post-16 campus at Les Ozouets.
Proposed by Taylor/St Pier so that more details of the proposed £35m. investment in regeneration of the Bridge come back to the States.
Proposed by Helyar/Ferbrache as a backstop to limit spending to only criticial investment should not new revenue raising measures be approved.
P&R proposed increasing personal income tax to 22%, but that was rejected in favour of a package of measures including GST being introduced in time for 2027.
Proposed by Roffey/Ferbrache to get the original 2023 GST package back on the agenda.
Proposed by Roffey/Ferbrache to get the original 2023 GST package back on the agend.
Proposed by Helyar/Matthews to hold the Budget at 2024 levels (£39.7m. less than proposed), delete vacant roles and employ new staff on static rates of pay.
Proposed by Parkinson/McKenna in support of introducing a territorital system of corporate tax from 2026.
Proposed by Prow/Inder for a two thirds majority to be needed to increase GST.
Proposed by Burford/de Sausmarez so proposals to address the deficit would have to be back to the States by March 2026, not September.
Proposed by Trott/Soulsby so that both short term income tax rises and planning for a GST to be in place by 2027 are options at the same time.
Proposed by Prow/Inder to ensure an examination of zero rating food under a GST regime.
Proposed by Trott/Soulsby to consult on changes to employee terms and condition changes rather than introduce them as proposed in amendment 3.
